Q1: I usually organized my study routine around listening rather than reading. I'd set aside blocks of time, maybe around 24 hours a week total, where I'd listen to recorded lectures or have my notes read aloud to me while I did other things. I found that I absorbed information better when I could hear it, so I'd often record myself summarizing concepts and play them back. My schedule wasn't always consistent with attending live sessions, but I made sure to catch up through audio materials.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was going on. I tried to keep some regularity, but with my extracurricular activities, sometimes I'd have to shift things around. When deadlines approached, I'd intensify my listening sessions and spend more time with audio recordings. I found that I could be flexible because my learning style allowed me to study while commuting or doing chores.
Q3: Honestly, I logged into the LMS mainly when I needed to check for new announcements or download materials I could use offline. Sometimes I'd go in to participate in discussions because I actually enjoyed connecting with my peers there. But I wasn't someone who logged in daily just to browse—I had specific purposes when I went in.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became more routine, especially early in the semester when everything was new. I'd log in to see if there were new discussion threads or if classmates had responded to my posts. But it never became a deeply ingrained habit because I found I could do most of my actual learning offline with downloaded materials and audio recordings.
Q5: The discussion forums were genuinely useful to me because I learn well through conversation and hearing different perspectives. I posted quite a bit and interacted with my peers regularly—it helped me process ideas by explaining them to others. The video lectures were also helpful, though I often preferred to download them and listen to the audio portion while doing other activities.
Q6: I tended to ignore the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources unless I absolutely had to use them. Reading walls of text just doesn't stick with me the way listening does. I also didn't complete all the small tasks and assignments because they felt less impactful than focusing on the material I knew would be on exams. My assignment completion wasn't great, but when I did submit work, I put real effort into it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it helped me actually understand concepts rather than just check a box. Discussion posts felt valuable because I was engaging with real ideas and real people. If something felt like busywork or didn't connect to the bigger picture of what I was learning, I'd deprioritize it in favor of deeper study methods that worked for me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like opportunities rather than obligations, which made me more selective about them. I'd engage with optional discussions if the topic interested me, but I wouldn't force myself through optional readings. Required activities sometimes felt tedious, especially if they didn't match my learning style, but I recognized I had to do them even if they weren't my preferred way of learning.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and convert them to audio files I could listen to anywhere. I also made my own voice memos summarizing key concepts and would listen to those repeatedly. I used external podcasts and YouTube videos related to course topics too—anything auditory that could reinforce what I was learning.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures on my phone during commutes or while exercising. I'd have study sessions with classmates where we'd discuss concepts verbally, which is how I learn best. None of that shows up as LMS activity, but it was real, meaningful learning.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or resource once, download it, and then work with it extensively offline. I might watch a lecture video once online and then listen to it three or four more times as audio while going about my day. The LMS would only show that single initial access, not all the learning that followed.
Q12: There were times when my extracurricular commitments got intense and I had to make choices about where to put my energy. I also went through a CBT session during the course, which was helpful for managing my overall wellbeing but took time and mental energy. During those periods, I'd focus on the essentials and let some of the smaller tasks slide.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was busy with extracurriculars or personal things, I'd minimize my time on the platform and rely more on offline studying. Fatigue sometimes made it hard to engage with dense written materials, so I'd switch to audio learning instead. I found ways to adapt rather than just pushing through ineffectively.
Q14: Sometimes I'd log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. It usually happened when I was tired or distracted, and I was just going through the motions. I learned to recognize when that was happening and would step away to study in a way that actually worked for me, like listening to recordings instead.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was that my assignment completion rate wasn't great—I'd prioritize the ones I felt confident about and delay others. Sometimes I avoided tasks that required a lot of reading because I knew they'd take me longer and feel more draining than audio-based learning.
Q16: Deadlines created bursts of activity for me. I'd increase my study intensity as deadlines approached, though not always in ways visible on the LMS. I'd cram listening sessions and review my audio notes more frequently. The LMS might show me logging in more often near deadlines, but the real work was happening offline.
Q17: Feedback did motivate me when it was constructive and specific. When I got good feedback on assignments, it encouraged me to put similar effort into future ones. My assignment scores were actually quite high when I did submit work, which reinforced that my methods were effective even if my completion rate wasn't perfect.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I appreciated. Having materials available to download meant I could adapt them to my learning style. However, the structure also assumed a lot of reading and text-based engagement, which wasn't ideal for me. I had to create my own parallel structure that worked better with how I learn.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement easier because I could extract the audio. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged real conversation, which I valued. What made it harder was when important information was only in written format with no audio alternative—I'd have to spend extra time converting or finding other ways to absorb it.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or looking for resources without necessarily producing output immediately. They could also be struggling to connect their activity to actual assignments, or they might be using the LMS as a starting point but doing their real work elsewhere. High activity doesn't always translate to completed submissions.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was relatively low, but I passed the course and did well on assignments I submitted. I was learning effectively through methods that didn't register on the platform—listening to recordings, discussing with peers in person, using external resources. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and timing, which might indicate when students are active. They can track discussion participation and assignment submissions accurately. But they miss all the offline learning, the quality of engagement versus just clicking through, and the different learning styles that don't fit the platform's design. They're a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively processing and connecting with the material in ways that lead to real understanding. For me, that's listening, discussing, and explaining concepts to others. It's not about how many times I log in or how long I stare at a screen—it's about whether I'm genuinely thinking about and working with the ideas.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent listening to downloaded materials, my conversations with classmates about course concepts, or the voice memos I made for myself. It wouldn't capture that I attended a CBT session that helped me manage my stress and stay balanced. It also wouldn't show that my learning style is auditory, which explains why my engagement looked different from students who learn by reading.
